serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sweet and sour pork cutlet in Singapore is approximately 200 g, representing one plate as commonly served at a hawker centre or Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and Sour Pork Cutlet — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Sweet and Sour Pork Cutlet (cr_id F06100050). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork, cooked, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered and sauced pork dish with moderate carbohydrate from batter and sweet sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour pork cutlet in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous battered and sauced pork dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (11 g/100g) from refined flour batter and sweet sauce. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 11.03 × 2) / 100 = 12.1, rounded to 12.2. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with the mixed macronutrient profile (high protein and fat moderating glucose response).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 668 mg/100g — exceeds 600 mg/100g hard threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish as its sodium content of 668 mg/100g exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper limit; request reduced sauce and avoid additional soy sauce or condiments to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined batter carbohydrate, added sugar in sauce, and deep-frying elevate glycaemic and fat load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish due to moderate carbohydrate (11 g/100g) from refined batter, 5 g/100g added sugar in the sweet and sour sauce, very low dietary fibre (0.75 g/100g), and high fat from deep-frying; request grilled or baked pork without batter and minimise sauce quantity to reduce glycaemic and caloric impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (668 mg/100g), deep-frying fat load, and saturated fat from pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (668 mg/100g), elevated total fat from deep-frying (10.86 g/100g), and saturated fat from pork, all of which contribute to elevated blood pressure and atherogenic risk; choose grilled lean pork preparations with low-sodium sauce instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; pork contributes arachidonic acid; bell peppers are nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation, pork contributes pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid (omega-6), and bell peppers as nightshade vegetables may exacerbate RA flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Pork Cutlet is a popular Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of deep-fried battered pork pieces coated in a glossy sweet and sour sauce, typically incorporating pineapple chunks and green bell pepper slices. The pork is first tenderised, coated in a seasoned batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a sauce made from vinegar, sugar, tomato ketchup, and pineapple juice. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, tender pork interior, and a vibrant tangy-sweet flavour profile balanced by the acidity of pineapple and the mild bitterness of green pepper. It is commonly served at hawker centres, Chinese restaurants, and zi char stalls across Singapore, and is a staple of Cantonese-influenced Singaporean cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 214 kcal, 17.96 g protein, 10.86 g fat, and 11.03 g carbohydrate with 5 g sugar and only 0.75 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 668 mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The deep-frying method increases total fat and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are relevant for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The moderate carbohydrate load from batter and sauce sugar, combined with low fibre, creates a low glycaemic impact relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. This dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100050) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pork cooked data as HPB does not report this breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific sweet and sour pork cutlet preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked pork (NDB 10219) and is documented as an adaptation. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable battered and sauced pork dishes; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved GI database. All core macros are non-zero and sourced from HPB. This dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets.
